definition: Primary judgment: whether the two adjacent units form a rhetorically relevant isocolonic or formally parallel pair. A colon need not be an independent clause; it may be a phrase, fragment, short answer, predicate, or dialogue turn if it functions as a member of the figure.
yes_or_high_rule: Use yes for a recognizable parallel pair that plausibly does rhetorical work.
no_or_low_rule: Use no for accidental similarity, exact repetition, formatting artifacts, merely formulaic dialogue, or uninterpretable fragments.
notes: Use uncertain when context, segmentation, or redaction blocks a stable judgment.

definition: Degree of matched form between unit1_text and unit2_text, including phonological, morphological, syntactic, lexical, or constructional matching.
yes_or_high_rule: Strong means clear matched frame, balanced units, repeated syntactic pattern, obvious slot substitution, or converging formal cues.
no_or_low_rule: None means no systematic matching; weak means only local similarity.
notes: Exact repetition can be strong here but still not count as human_isocolonic.

definition: Whether the parallelism or relation appears to help organize, contrast, compare, coordinate, clarify, identify, intensify, or make memorable the discourse.
yes_or_high_rule: Use yes if the form or relation contributes to the rhetorical function.
no_or_low_rule: Use no if similarity is only formatting, repetition, metadata, repair, or accidental short-unit matching.
notes: Apposition or identity can be rhetorically relevant even when it is not comparison and not isocolon.

definition: Whether the pair sets two items, alternatives, actions, qualities, or states beside each other.
yes_or_high_rule: Use yes for lists, contrasts, disjunctions, comparisons, and antitheses.
no_or_low_rule: Use no for pure repetition, restatement, attribution, phatic exchange, apposition/identity, or elaboration without a paired contrast/list.
notes: This tracks the theoretical target, not just surface form; yes here does not force human_isocolonic yes.

definition: Whether high score is mainly due to exact or near-exact repetition.
yes_or_high_rule: Use yes for Thank you/Thank you, repeated names, repeated fragments, capitalization variants.
no_or_low_rule: Use no when the pair uses repeated structure with meaningful slot substitution.
notes: A repeated frame is not automatically a false positive.

definition: Whether high score is mainly due to headings, list entries, labels, proof bullets, or menu-like formatting.
yes_or_high_rule: Use yes when formatting rather than wording creates the match.
no_or_low_rule: Use no when list wording itself creates a rhetorical parallel pair.

definition: Whether high score is mainly due to formulaic dialogue, backchannel, repair, short answer, or turn-taking.
yes_or_high_rule: Use yes for ordinary short dialogue turns that look parallel only because they are short or formulaic.
no_or_low_rule: Use no when dialogue contains a clear rhetorical parallel pair or meaningful dialogic echo.
notes: Dialogue genre is not by itself a false positive; dialogic echo can count.

definition: Whether a template, boilerplate, copied title pattern, or quoted source is the main reason for the match.
yes_or_high_rule: Use yes when template/quotation makes the current relation a poor test of rhetorical parallelism.
no_or_low_rule: Use no when the quoted language itself is the rhetorical figure being evaluated.
notes: Explain quoted genuine cases in notes.

definition: Whether the relation arguments are split so badly that the pair cannot be judged.
yes_or_high_rule: Use yes for stray complements, broken constructions, or crucial material outside the row.
no_or_low_rule: Use no when the pair is interpretable as given.
notes: Use uncertain for heavy redaction or markup.

definition: Numeric confidence in the primary code.
notes: Confidence is for the primary human_isocolonic judgment.

yes_or_high_rule: State what made the case count or not count.
notes: Mention repetition, list formatting, dialogue formula, quotation, bad segmentation, or genuine slot substitution.
